hero_image.caption: H. T. Kealing.
hero_image.photographer: Unknown (portrait as published in 'The Negro Problem', 1903)
hero_image.repository: HathiTrust Digital Library; from 'The Negro Problem' (1903)
hero_image.license: Public domain in the United States (published 1903, before January 1, 1931)

inline_images.alt: Reverend Henry Hugh Proctor, pastor of the First Congregational Church, Atlanta, Georgia, 1900
inline_images.caption: The Reverend Henry Hugh Proctor of Atlanta, photographed in 1900, reflecting the church leadership and religious life central to H. T. Kealing's account of the inner character of Black Americans.
inline_images.photographer: African American Photographs Assembled for the 1900 Paris Exposition (Daniel Murray Collection)
inline_images.repository: Library of Congress, Prints and Photographs Division

related_chapters.subject: Mildred Pitts Walter
related_chapters.chapter_title: Beautiful Because Teacher Told Me So
related_chapters.why: Kealing catalogs self-respect amid abasement as a defining trait, and Walter speaks on natural hair and Black pride, on being taught to see oneself as beautiful.

related_chapters.subject: Euvester Simpson
related_chapters.topic: A Washcloth and a Gospel Song
related_chapters.why: Kealing names deep religiousness and endurance under suffering as inborn characteristics, and Simpson recalls a gospel song that carried her through the night in the Winona jail.

related_chapters.subject: Roberta Alexander
related_chapters.part_title: Reflections on a Life in Movements
related_chapters.chapter_title: I Walk Through the World as African American
related_chapters.why: The essay turns on how the race understands its own identity, and Alexander reflects directly on identity and belonging, on walking through the world as African American.

related_chapters.subject: Candie Carawan and Guy Hughes Carawan
related_chapters.chapter_title: Building a Beloved Community Through Song
related_chapters.why: Kealing singles out the imaginative, musical character of the people and the songs born of hard occasions, and the Carawans describe building community through the freedom-song tradition.

related_chapters.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
related_chapters.chapter_title: Look White Men in the Eye
related_chapters.why: Kealing weighs self-respect against the deference slavery bred, and the Ladners recount a family lesson to look white men in the eye, a refusal of the inherited posture of submission.

related_chapters.subject: William Saunders
related_chapters.why: The essay frames the people's struggle upward as a matter of self-worth, and Saunders recounts the origin of the You Are Somebody message during the Charleston hospital strike.

top_voice.subject: Mildred Pitts Walter
top_voice.reason: Kealing's essay anatomizes the inner character and self-respect of the people, and Walter, who later wrote children's books so the young could see themselves, speaks to that interior sense of worth in her account of Black pride.

closing_image.alt: Entrance to Southern University and A&M College in Baton Rouge, Louisiana
closing_image.caption: Entrance to Southern University and A&M College in Baton Rouge, Louisiana. Mildred Pitts Walter is the corpus voice this essay connects to most.

connections.summary: Across the Civil Rights History Project, the oral histories below take up the questions The Characteristics of the Negro People raises. Our embedding model places it nearest the testimony of Myrtle Gonza Glascoe, Amos C. Brown, and Harold K. Brown.
